coredata.dc:title: Preparation of magnesium aminoclay-carbon dots/TiO2 as photocatalysts for wastewater management
coredata.dc:creator: Bui, Vu Khac Hoang
Koh, Jin-Soo
Lee, Hyun Uk
Park, Duckshin
Lee, Young-Chul
coredata.dc:description: Magnesium aminoclay (MgAC) has been used to remove different harmful pollutants from the environment. In this study, MgAC was successfully converted into MgAC-carbon dots (MgAC-CDs) using microwave radiation, which was then used to prepare MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites for use in water treatment. Pure TiO2, MgAC-CDs, and MgAC-CDs/TiO2 were investigated via X-ray powder diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller theory, and photoluminescence. Through these techniques, the presence of MgAC-CDs decreased the size of TiO2, and the interaction between TiO2 and MgAC-CDs led to the formation of vacant oxygen in TiO2. MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 had the best photocatalytic performance in the ultraviolet spectrum/light-emitting diode (LED, in visible spectrum) among all MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites. Preliminary data indicate that a concentration of 1 mg/mL can inactivate an MS2 bacteriophage in 1 h of irradiation under ultraviolet light or LEDs. The high photocatalytic performances of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 can be explained by interactions between MgAC-CDs and TiO2, which forms a layer of vacant oxygen, and also by the remaining MgAC sheet, which contributes to the high specific surface area.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnesium aminoclay-carbon dots
TiO2
Photocatalyst
MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites
Water treatment

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Land Infrastructure and Transport, Republic of Korea
National Research Facilities and Equipment Center

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by a grant from the Subway Fine Dust Reduction Technology Development Project of the Ministry of Land Infrastructure and Transport, Republic of Korea ( 21QPPW-B152306-03 ), and by the Basic Science Research Capacity Enhancement Project, through a Korea Basic Science Institute ( National Research Facilities and Equipment Center ) grant funded by the Ministry of Education ( 2019R1A6C1010016 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a and b) HR TEM images of MgAC-CDs, and (c) XRD pattern of MgAC-CDs.

(a) Photocatalytic activities of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites under UV irradiation, (b) LED irradiation, and (c) the photocatalytic activities of the MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 sample in the absence of scavengers and the presence of methanol (in red), isopropanol (in blue), and p-benzoquinone (in green). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Inactivation performances of MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 composites against MS2 bacteriophage under different conditions.
XRD of MgAC-CDs and MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites.
TEM (a), HR TEM (b), and SAED (c) images of MgAC-CDs/TiO2.
XPS spectra of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites: (a) full-scale XPS spectrum, and the corresponding (b) C1s, (c) O1s, and (d) Ti2p.
Suggested photocatalytic mechanism of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composite interactions.
Pseudo-first-order rate constants of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites under UV and visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Preparation of magnesium aminoclay-carbon dots/TiO2 as photocatalysts for wastewater management

Corresponding author. Korea Railroad Research Institute (KRRI), 176 Cheoldobakmulkwan-ro, Uiwang-si, 16105, Gyeonggi-do, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnesium aminoclay (MgAC) has been used to remove different harmful pollutants from the environment. In this study, MgAC was successfully converted into MgAC-carbon dots (MgAC-CDs) using microwave radiation, which was then used to prepare MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites for use in water treatment. Pure TiO2, MgAC-CDs, and MgAC-CDs/TiO2 were investigated via X-ray powder diffraction, transmission electron microscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Brunauer–Emmett–Teller theory, and photoluminescence. Through these techniques, the presence of MgAC-CDs decreased the size of TiO2, and the interaction between TiO2 and MgAC-CDs led to the formation of vacant oxygen in TiO2. MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 had the best photocatalytic performance in the ultraviolet spectrum/light-emitting diode (LED, in visible spectrum) among all MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites. Preliminary data indicate that a concentration of 1 mg/mL can inactivate an MS2 bacteriophage in 1 h of irradiation under ultraviolet light or LEDs. The high photocatalytic performances of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 can be explained by interactions between MgAC-CDs and TiO2, which forms a layer of vacant oxygen, and also by the remaining MgAC sheet, which contributes to the high specific surface area.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The Earth currently suffers from severe water shortage and pollution caused by wastewater from industrial development, reckless exploitation of groundwater, and miscellaneous environmental pollution from human activity. Consequently, 17% of the world's population does not have access to clean drinking water, resulting in 4,500 children dying every day [1]. Dyes, organic compounds, and phenolic compounds have been detected in wastewater that is discharged during industrial development, which is both toxic and difficult to control naturally; this poses a threat to both the surrounding environment and to humans [1–4]. Owing to the hazardous effects of polluted water, strategies to efficiently remove toxic compounds from wastewater are in great demand. Conventionally, approaches to treat wastewater include chemical precipitation, electrolysis, ion exchange adsorption, biological treatment technology, and Fenton oxidation treatment technology; however, these methods are ineffective because of their slow reaction rate, high cost, and low efficacy [1,4,5]. To circumvent these problems, photocatalysts have been studied for decades. Research has been conducted on novel photocatalyst materials (e.g., Cds, GaP, Fe2O3, and TiO2) that remove toxic compounds from wastewater. TiO2 is the most popular of these examples, owing to its properties such as low toxicity, high chemical stability, and low cost overall [6,7]. Despite this popularity, TiO2 has limitations, including a large bandgap (i.e., 3.2 eV) and the fast recombination of photoexcited electrons and holes [8]. Different strategies to overcome these limitations include decoration with noble metals, doping with metal/nonmetal elements, sensitization with organic dyes, and coupling with increasingly narrow bandgap semiconductors [8]. The combination of TiO2 and quantum/carbon dots has recently proven to shift photocatalytic activation of TiO2 into the range of visible light irradiation [8,9]. Yu et al. (2014) found that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) play two roles in the increase the photocatalytic activity of TiO2: 1) under ultraviolet (UV) light irradiation, CQDs act as electron reservoirs; 2) under visible light irradiation, CQDs act as photosensitizers which sensitize Degussa P25 (commercial TiO2) into a visible light-response “dyade” structure [8]. Another study found that the CQDs/BiVO4 composite has high photocatalytic activity under the influence of upconverted photoluminescence (PL), photo-induced electron transfer, and a CQDs reservoir in the presence of simulated solar light [10]. Low photosensitization efficiency remains a significant limitation of using CQDs [11]; nitrogen doping is considered to improve the photocatalytic performance of composites containing CQDs [11].

Magnesium aminoclay (MgAC) is 2-D material with a unique layered structure and high dispersity in water owing to its propylamine groups [12–14]. MgAC has been used to remove different types of heavy metals and organic dyes from wastewater, owing to its high adsorptive properties [15]. MgAC has also been conjugated with various materials, including nZVI, Fe3O4, and TiO2, for environmental-treatment purposes [16–20]. In the recently published paper, we found that tin-aminoclay (SnAC) becomes partially converted to SnAC-dots after microwave treatment. Additional calcination treatments in the presence of Ar revealed that the SnO2-SnAC/reduced graphene oxide (SnO2-SnAC/rGO) composite performs well as an anode for Li-ion batteries (LIBs), with a capacity of 650 mAh g−1 after 100 cycles at 100 mA g−1, along with high rate capability [21]. This performance provides an opportunity to develop different composites based on aminoclay dots for various applications. In this study, we created MgAC-carbon dots (CDs)/TiO2 composites based on MgAC-CDs to improve the efficiency of water treatment processes. MgAC-CDs are considered to increase the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 by shifting its photocatalytic activation point into the visible wavelength range (400–700 nm). As MgAC is not fully converted to MgAC-CDs, its layered structure plays an important role in the adsorption of pollutant molecules.

Titanium (Ⅳ) butoxide (TB, 97%) and methylene blue (MB) powder was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Ethyl alcohol (EtOH; 18 L, 94.5%) was obtained from Daejunghwageum Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Korea), and magnesium chloride hexahydrate (MgCl2●6H2O, ≥ 99%) was purchased from Junsei Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). 3-aminopropyltrielthoxylane (APTES, ≥ 98%) was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). In all experiments conducted during this study, distilled water (DI) that was purified using a Milli-Q purification process (Millipore, MA, USA) was used.

MgAC was synthesized according to the methodology described in previous studies [16]. Briefly, 1.68 g of MgCl2●6H2O was completely dissolved in 40 mL of EtOH, after which 2 mL of APTES was added; the mixture was stirred for 8 h to generate a white suspension. The resulting suspension was centrifuged (3,500 rpm/15 min), and washed thrice with 50 mL of ethanol to remove residues. After ethanol washing, the suspension was dried at 40 °C and subsequently ground into powder using a mortar and pestle.

In a crucible containing 10 mL of DI water, 1 g of MgAC was dissolved by stirring for 10 min at room temperature. The MgAC solution was then heated in the microwave for 2 min at 800 W. After heating, the crucible was cooled at room temperature. MgAC-CDs were then obtained by grinding the contents of the crucible using a mortar and pestle.

The MgAC-CDs prepared through the aforementioned process were dissolved in a beaker containing 40 mL of EtOH in quantities of 0.05 g, 0.1 g, 0.2 g, and 0.3 g, and these mixtures were stirred at room temperature for 30 min. Following this, 1 mL of TB was slowly added and stirred for 10 min. After 10 min, 25
μ
L of DI water was added to the mixture, which was then stirred for 12 h. The resulting solution was poured into the conical tube and centrifuged at 3500 rpm for 15 min, after which the separated components were washed with EtOH and dried in an oven at 60 °C for 24 h. When the resulting substance was dry, it was finely ground using a mortar and pestle, and was put into a crucible. The obtained powder was then calcined at 350 °C for 3 h by the muffle furnace to achieve MgAC-CDs/TiO2 hybrid nanocomposites [16,19].

The MgAC-CDs/TiO2 sample was added to a petri dish containing 100 mL of methylene blue (MB; 10 ppm) solution using 0.1 g of the sample. After the equilibrium adsorption was reached in a dark environment, an UV lamp (6 W) with 365 nm wavelength or a light-emitting diode (LED) lamp was turned on. Samples were removed from the light every 20 min and were centrifuged. MB concentration in withdrawal samples was observed via UV–Vis spectroscopy (Cary 50-UV Vis Spectrophotometers, Varian Inc., USA), at a wavelength of 664 nm. The rate of decomposition of organic compounds is determined by the pseudo-first-order rate equation [22]:
1
d
[ C ]
d t
= k
[
C o
]
where C
o is the initial concentration of MB, C is the concentration of MB after time t, and k is the pseudo-first-order rate constant (in min−1).

For recycling performance, at initial concentration of MB (10 ppm), 0.1 g of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 was injected. After 30 min stirring under dark condition, UV of 365 nm wavelength was irradiated. After completing 2 h reaction, by centrifugation, the dye concentration in the supernatant was measured. The precipitate of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 was recycled. Again, after adjusting initial MB concentration, recycling test was repeated [23].

MS2 bacteriophage (Escherichia coli (E.coli) bacteriophage MS2, American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) 15597-B1; Manassas, VA, USA) was used as the model virus in the antivirus experiments in this study, much like in several pre-existing studies [24,25]. The MS2 bacteriophages were activated by adding 100 μL of stock MS2 and 300 μL of a late-exponential or early-stationary-phase E. coli (host) into 25 mL of LB media and incubating the mixture overnight. MS2 was collected from the supernatant of the mixture after centrifuging at 4,000 rpm for 20 min [24].

For our antivirus experiment, 100 μL of activated MS2 (i.e., 105–106 PFU/mL) was added to Petri dishes containing 50 mL of MgAC-CDs/TiO2, at a concentration of 1 mg/mL; Petri dishes were subsequently irradiated with UV light or LEDs. An antivirus experiment without UV/LED light irradiation was also conducted as a control. Samples were withdrawn after every hour and were centrifuged to eliminate the MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites. MS2 concentration in the withdrawn samples was counted using plaque counting methods. The serially diluted samples were separated into quantities of 100 μL and were mixed with pre-culture host (300 μL) and 10 mL of trypticase soy agar (TSA). The resulting mixture was then placed on a 90-mm (diameter) petri dish, incubated overnight at 37 °C, and counted [25]. The virus survival rate (in %) was calculated using the following equation:
2
S u r v i v a l
r a t e
( % )
=
C t
C o
× 100
where C
o is the initial concentration of the virus, and C
t is the concentration of the virus at time t.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 was conducted using a Rigaku D/max-2500 (18 kW, Japan), incorporating a θ/θ goniometer equipped with a 40 kV, 30 mA CuKα radiation generator. The crystallite size of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 was calculated by using Scherrer's formula [26]:
3
D ⋅ = ⋅
K λ
β
c o s θ
where K is Scherrer's constant (i.e., 0.89), λ is the wavelength of X-ray sources (i.e., 0.15405 nm), β is the peak Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM), and θ is the angle at peak position.

The morphology of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites was characterized via transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEM-2100F, JEOL Ltd., Peabody, MA, USA) abd scanning electron microscopy (SEM; Hitachi S4700, Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; ES-CALAB250, VG scientific, East Grinstead, UK) was used to investigate the chemical and electronic states of the elements in the composites. The pore structure of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites was investigated using Micromeritics TriStar II (Micromeritics Instrument Corporation, USA). For the photocurrent density, photo-electrodes with TiO2 or MgAC-CDs/TiO2 spin coated on fluoride doped tin oxide-coated (FTO) glass substrate (0.1 cm2) and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene):poly(styrene sulfonate) (PEDOT:PSS) as top electrode were prepared. 365 nm (UV-A) were used as light sources with power density of 0.8 mW/cm−2. Electron impedence spectra (EIS) were recorded with using a Solartron 1260A impedance analyzer in the frequency range of 100 KHz to 0.1 Hz under the irriation of Xenon lamp [27].

High-resolution (HR) TEM images (Fig. 1 a) indicate that MgAC was converted to MgAC-CDs. The size of CDs was 2.99 ± 0.68 nm with a lattice spacing of ∼0.208 nm, which could be assigned to the (1 0 2) plane of graphitic (sp2) carbon (Fig. 1b) [9]. The formation of CDs on the surface of MgAC could be attributed to the decomposition of propylamine groups, similar to the report from Nelson et al. (2016), which used APTES to form carbon dots on the surface of mesoporous silica particles [28]. XRD patterns (see Fig. 1c) indicate that the presence of MgAC-CDs does not change the amorphous properties of MgAC; however, by converting MgAC to MgAC-CDs, more blue light was emitted after excitation due to UV-365 nm light (see Fig. S1a). In addition to blue light emissions, the MgAC-CDs emit green light and red light after excitation by 420 nm and 460 nm wavelengths, respectively (see Fig. S1b) [29]. N1s XPS signal of MgAC-CDs indicated the presence of pyrrolic N and graphitic N at 399.41 and 401.2 eV, respectively (see Fig. S1c) [11]. The appereance of these N-doping states, particularly pyrrolic N, could explain the high blue emission of MgAC-CDs [11]. Results confirmed that amino-propyl groups of the original aminoclay could act as nitrogen sources during the synthesis of MgAC-CDs. MgAC-CDs with high photoluminescence (PL) can be used as efficient photosensitizers for photocatalysts composites [11].

The photocatalytic mechanisms of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 are suggested by following equation:
4 MgAC-CDs/TiO2 + hv (>3.2 eV) → MgAC-CDs/TiO2 (e− + h+)
5 h+ + H2O → H+ + ●OH
6 h+ + OH− → ●OH
7 O2 + e− → ●O2 −
8
●HO2 + O2 − + h+ → H2O2
9 H2O2 → 2●OH
10
●OH + MB → MB+
11 MB+ + 2e− + H+ → leucomethylene blue

To investigate the contribution of reactive oxygen species (ROS) to the photocatalytic performance of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites, different scavengers have been added to the photocatalytic reaction: isopropanol for ●OH, methanol for both h+ and ●OH, and p-benzoquinone for ●O2 − [32,33]. According to Fig. 2c, it can be concluded that ●OH primarily drives the photocatalytic performance of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composite, rather than the valance band h+ and the conduction band e− [32]. Terephthalic acid (TA) fluorescence test was additionally conducted to confirm the presence of ●OH [34,35]. At the concentration of terephthalic acid (1 mM) in 100 mL solution, 0.1 g of MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 was injected. After 30 min stirring under dark condition, UV of 365 nm wavelength was irradiated. After 30 min reaction, by centrifugation, the supernatant was scanned by Microplate reader (Synergy H-1M, Bio Tek Instruments, USA) at excitation of 315 nm and emission of 425 nm. The control sample in the absence of MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 was also conducted [35]. Due to the presence of MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2, the combination of TA and ●OH resulted in the formation of 2-hydroxyterephththalic acid (TAOH) which emitted high fluorescence intensity at 425 nm (see Fig. S3).

Based on photocatalytic performance of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 for MB degradation, we selected MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 for their antivirus experiment. Results indicated that MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 at 1 mg/mL can inactivate MS2 even without the irradiation of UV/LED. The survival rate of MS2 bacteriophages without irradiation of UV/LED was 27.03 ± 3.5 (%) after 3 h. However, with the irradiation of UV/LED, MS2 is nearly inactivated after 1 h. The MS2 survival rates in the case of UV irradiation and LED irradiation are 6.62 ± 3.74 (%) and 17.27 ± 1.21 (%), respectively (see Fig. 3 ). In future studies, rather than bacteriophage, the inactivation of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 will be extended to harmful human virus such as Influenza A virus subtype H1N1 or severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2).

XRD patterns showed that the TiO2 anatase peaks at 2θ = 25.3° (1 0 1), 37.9° (0 0 4), 48.0° (2 0 0), 54.0° (1 0 5), 54.9° (2 1 1), and 62.7° (2 0 4) for TiO2, MgAC-CDs [0.05 g]/TiO2, and MgAC-CDs [0.10 g]/TiO2 samples, respectively. The intensity of TiO2 anatase decreased with the increase in MgAC-CDs concentration and disappeared completely in MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 and MgAC-CDs [0.30 g]/TiO2 samples. It can thus be concluded that the TiO2 in MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2, and MgAC-CDs [0.30 g]/TiO2 samples is amorphous or hidden (see Fig. 4 ). For pure TiO2, MgAC-CDs [0.05 g]/TiO2, and MgAC-CDs [0.10 g]/TiO2 samples, the TiO2 crystalline size was 13.27 nm, 10.08 nm, and 9.26 nm, respectively, according to Scherrer's equation (see Table S2; Eq. (3) ). It can be recognized that the increase in MgAC-CDs concentrations leads to a decrease in the crystalline size of TiO2. For the MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 sample, the average crystalline size of TiO2 was 8.63 nm, according to TEM images (see Fig. 5 a). Owing to the decrease in crystalline size, TiO2 is more easily aggregated into the microsphere-like structure (see Fig. S4) [36]. Due to the decrease of diffraction peaks, as mentioned earlier, the presence of MgAC and MgAC-CDs can constrain the crystal growth or enhance the disorder-elicited lattice strains [30,37]. In addition, CDs on the surface of MgAC can play as the reducing agent for the formation of nonstoichiometric TiO2 (Eq. 12–13) and oxygen vacancies [38–40]. A lattice spacing of 0.352 nm, belonging to the (1 0 1) of anatase TiO2, which could be assigned to the (1 0 2) planes of graphitic (sp2) carbon at 0.208 nm, thus confirmed the existence of these particles in the composite (see Fig. 5b) [9,41]. The concentric ring of selected area electron diffraction (SAED) confirmed the formation of anatase TiO2 in the MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 sample (see Fig. 5c). From TEM images, it can be seen that TiO2 NPs were aggregated into bigger particles with the size of 105 ± 34.97 nm, while the layered sheet of MgAC remained intact.
12 TiO2 + xC → TiO2-x + xCO
13 TiO2 + xCO → TiO2-x + xCO2

XPS was used to investigate the interactions between TiO2 and MgAC-CDs. The full XPS spectrum indicated the existence of Mg, O, Ti, C, and Si in the MgAC-CDs/TiO2 samples (see Fig. 6 a). The C (1s) spectrum can be deconvoluted into 284.96, 286.46, and 288.10 eV, which are assigned to the C–C (graphite), C–O, and CO/CN, respectively (see Fig. 6b); the O (1s) spectrum can be deconvoluted into 530.42, 531.57, 532.28, and 533.24 eV, which are attributed to the Ti–O, O–H, C–O, and oxygen vacancy (Vo) atoms, respectively (see Fig. 6c). These results suggest the presence of MgAC-CDs in the MgAC-CDs/TiO2 samples and the connection of MgAC-CDs and TiO2 via the Ti–O–C bond [42,43]. Compared to pure TiO2, the peaks at 464.61 and 458.86 eV for Ti(2p1/2) and Ti(2p3/2) of MgAC-CDs/TiO2, respectively, shifted to slightly lower binding energies due to charge redistribution between the O and Ti atoms due to the formation of oxygen vacancies and Ti3+ [44] (see Fig. 6d). The presence of oxygen vacancies and Ti3+ could be explained for the formation of nonstoichiometric TiO2 [45,46]. The introduction of MgAC-CDs into TiO2 can lead to the formation of the Vo layer and prevent the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes under photoexcitation, thus increasing the photocatalytic performance of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites and shifting its photocatalytic activation into the visible range [44]. The optical absorption of MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 composite was investigated by UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (see Fig. S5a). From the spectra, the absorption of MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 composites could be extended to visible light. The value of the bandgap can be additionally calculated from the UV–vis diffuse spectra (see Fig. S5b). The bandgap value of MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 was 2.24 eV. In addition, the maximum current density of MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 (1,295 μA/cm2 at 2.0 V) is much higher than pristine TiO2 (56.64 μA/cm2), indicating stronger ability of producing charge carriers and more separated electrons in MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 (see Fig. S6) [47]. Fig. S7 displayed the typical Nyquist impedance of TiO2 and MgAC-CDs/TiO2 samples. MgAC-CDs [0.20g]/TiO2 showed the lowest interfacial impedance than other samples, indicating an improvement of charge separation by the delaying of excited electron-hole recombination [48].

This study assumed that the photocatalytic mechanisms of MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites(see Fig. 7 ) could be beneficial for wastewater treatment processes. First, the stability of the MgAC sheet is essential for the adsorption of polluting reactants. Owing to the presence of MgAC-CDs, MgAC-CDs/TiO2 composites had considerably higher surface areas than TiO2 alone; the highest surface area was of MgAC-CDs [0.2 g]/TiO2 (i.e., 128.51 m2/g), thereby exhibiting the highest photocatalytic activity (see Table S2). All samples used in this study had well-developed mesoporous structures owing to the typical type IV isotherms with H3 hysteretic loops at the high relative pressure range of 0.4–1 (P/Po) (see Fig. S8) [30]. Second, similar to other CDs/TiO2 and N-doped CDs/TiO2 composites, MgAC-CDs can act as both photosensitizers and electron reservoirs in photocatalytic composites. Under UV/visible light irradiation, MgAC-CDs with N-doping states can suppress non-radiative quenching by reducing the inner-layer energy traps between sp2 domains, thereby leading to high PL intensity. Therefore, more photogenerated will be transferred from MgAC-CDs to the vacant oxygen layer of TiO2. In contrast, under UV light irradiation, the charge separation in TiO2 leads to a transfer of electrons to the absorbed MgAC-CDs, which serve as active sites for the reduction process [11].

In this study, MgAC was successfully converted to MgAC-CDs, with the crystalline size of CDs being 2.99 ± 0.68 nm. Characterization data indicated that MgAC-CDs have high fluorescence emissions related to the N-doped states originating from MgAC. Owing to high PL intensity, MgAC-CDs are well-suited to form composites with TiO2. Among the MgAC-CDs/TiO2 samples used in this study, MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 exhibited the maximum increase in photocatalytic activity under UV and visible light. Sample MgAC-CDs [0.20 g]/TiO2 can also inactivate MS2 bacteriophages in 1 h under UV or LED irradiation. The interactions between MgAC-CDs and TiO2 led to the formation of a layer of vacant oxygen, which can prevent the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes under photoexcitation, thereby increasing the photocatalytic performance of the composites and shifting it into the visible range; the unchanged MgAC sheet is beneficial for the adsorption of polluting reactants by increasing the specific surface area.

Vu Khac Hoang Bui: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – original draft. Jin-Soo Koh: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Hyun Uk Lee: Resources, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Duckshin Park: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Young-Chul Lee: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by a grant from the Subway Fine Dust Reduction Technology Development Project of the Ministry of Land Infrastructure and Transport, Republic of Korea (21QPPW-B152306-03), and by the Basic Science Research Capacity Enhancement Project, through a Korea Basic Science Institute (National Research Facilities and Equipment Center) grant funded by the Ministry of Education (2019R1A6C1010016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2022.126541.
